methodology: Use existing paper metadata only as a seed for candidate neighborhoods: Axis A/B placement, technologies, workloads, first-class objects, rewrite objects, artifact status, author/group fields, and public note summaries.
Before strengthening a cluster, inspect the actual corpus notes, paper claims, and official artifacts for the stack object, transformation path, backend handoff, and boundary of the claim.
Write cluster content as analysis paragraphs or prose graphs, not as prose descriptions of metadata clustering.

Treat working-group analysis as an independent lens over artifact lineage, authorship context, repositories, and institutional practice. It may intersect clusters, but it is not subordinate to cluster analysis.
Prefer coarse families with repeated stack objects or visible tool lineage over fine-grained author-network claims, and keep narrow clusters narrow when the binding object changes.
Mark uncertain boundaries directly and link back to scoped atlas views for inspection.

clusters.label: Crossbar DNN compiler and simulator toolchain line
clusters.analysis: This cluster is best described as a crossbar-DNN toolchain line whose reusable object is distributed across several adjacent handoff formats rather than concentrated in one formal IR. PIMCOMP makes array groups, structure/weight separation, AG-to-core mapping, HT/LL schedules, and pseudo-instructions first-class enough to compile ONNX-style DNNs into verification and simulation artifacts. PIMSIM-NN then treats the lowered per-core instruction stream plus architecture/NoC config as the simulator ABI. PIMSYN-NN sits one step earlier, synthesizing macro/PE/crossbar architecture and dataflow choices through an IR-based DAG and JSON configuration outputs, while PIMACC sits one step later, replaying PIMCOMP verification state under quantization, bit-slicing, conductance variation, IR-drop approximation, and stuck-at fault settings. The cluster boundary is therefore the visible compiler/synthesis/simulator artifact path for static crossbar CNN/DNN inference, not a broad claim about every crossbar paper or a full collaboration graph.
object_flow: Entry object: a DNN model graph, usually ONNX, JSON, or an equivalent graph representation.
Binding/rewrite: layers and weights are placed onto crossbar arrays, array groups, PIMFUs, cores, and chip-level configuration state.
Backend handoff: the durable object is an instruction stream, pseudo-instruction set, hardware configuration, or simulator-facing execution artifact.
Boundary: the toolchain continuity is visible, but paper-by-paper schema compatibility still needs artifact-level checking.

clusters.label: Digital SRAM-CIM macro generation and precision specialization
clusters.analysis: This cluster is strongest when read as a macro-materialization problem. AutoDCIM, ARCTIC, SynDCIM, and OpenC2 do not mainly ask how a neural network graph becomes an instruction stream; they ask how a digital SRAM-CIM macro specification becomes a concrete backend artifact. The repeated object is a configurable macro contract: rows and columns, weight and input bit widths, INT or FP format fields, SRAM and compute-cell choices, multiplier or mux structure, adder and accumulator topology, BIST/test modes, timing and area constraints, and the generator scripts or templates that turn those fields into RTL, netlists, DEF, GDSII, post-layout reports, or silicon-calibrated macro evidence. The analytical boundary is therefore lower than most model-compilation clusters: precision is a backend type parameter, subcircuit libraries are the costed vocabulary, and physical layout constraints are part of the compiler state rather than a postscript. OpenACMv2 belongs beside this cluster as a boundary case because it shares digital SRAM/DCIM backend objects, but its durable object is an accuracy-constrained approximate-arithmetic search trajectory, not the same spec-to-layout macro compiler lineage.
object_flow: Entry object: a macro specification names rows, columns, input and weight bit widths, INT/FP format fields, SRAM and compute-cell choices, and timing or area targets before workload scheduling becomes the dominant concern.

Binding/rewrite: the compiler state is specialized through template selection, array partitioning, shared-exponent placement, subcircuit-library search, adder or accumulator topology, BIST insertion, approximate compressor assignment, and EDA constraints.

Backend handoff: the durable object is generated RTL, Verilog or SPICE netlists, LEF/LIB-compatible cells, DEF/GDSII layout, timing/DRC/LVS reports, or silicon-calibrated macro evidence rather than a reusable model-graph dialect.

Boundary: the core cluster is spec/config-to-macro materialization; OpenACM-style approximate search is related because it shares DCIM backend objects, but it should remain a separate optimization boundary until stronger lineage evidence appears.

clusters.label: Analog CIM modeling, nonideality, and calibrated cost baselines
clusters.analysis: This cluster is best read as a set of backend modeling contracts rather than as one compiler stack. NeuroSim makes the device-to-architecture hierarchy first-class: memory-cell parameters, subarray dimensions, ADC/sensing choices, buffers, interconnect, traces, and layer/component reports become the surface that a future compiler would need to target. CiMLoop exposes a different but compatible boundary by turning Timeloop/Accelergy-style YAML/Jinja configs, container hierarchy, per-component reuse, encoding and bit slicing, operand distributions, plug-in cost models, and calibration scales into editable modeling state. GENIEx narrows the contract to analog fidelity: a voltage vector and conductance matrix produce a learned current-ratio correction that can be injected into PyTorch MVM layers, while NAX uses that nonideality feedback together with per-operation crossbar-size and cost-table choices during architecture search. NS-Cache is adjacent rather than central: it extends the NeuroSim-style device/circuit/cache cost stack toward cache hierarchy, refresh, and Gem5/Ruby timing contracts. The shared object is therefore not an IR dialect or application runtime, but a calibrated description of what happens when values pass through analog or memory-centric backend resources.
object_flow: Entry object: a model, workload trace, mapping candidate, or hardware configuration is projected onto device, circuit, array, tile, chip, Timeloop-style container, or cache hierarchy descriptions.

Binding/rewrite: the modeling layer makes subarray binding, ADC/DAC precision, sensing, bit slicing, operand distributions, crossbar size, nonideality transfer functions, calibration scales, and refresh or timing assumptions reusable state.
Backend handoff: the durable object is an accuracy, cost, mapping, timing, calibration, or simulator-parameter report that feeds architecture search, compiler comparison, or system simulation.
Boundary: the cluster is methodological; NeuroSim, CiMLoop, GENIEx/NAX, and NS-Cache share backend modeling objects, but they do not form one compiler lineage or one collaboration graph.

clusters.label: UPMEM real-hardware runtime and benchmark layer
clusters.analysis: This cluster is the real-hardware counterpart to the simulator and API clusters: its durable object is host-DPU execution state on commercial UPMEM systems. PrIM establishes the low-level contract by making DPU allocation, tasklet count, MRAM/WRAM staging, transfer size, host-mediated barriers, workload partitioning, and timing categories visible through benchmark implementations and profiling outputs. SimplePIM raises that contract into a programmer-facing runtime object, the PIM-resident array, where symbolic array IDs, per-DPU lengths, placement metadata, lazy zip provenance, function handles, map/reduce iterators, and host-emulated collectives carry the state that a compiler would otherwise need to synthesize. PIM-Opt and PIM-TC then show how workload-specific algorithms deepen the same boundary: distributed optimization becomes a synchronization trajectory over host parameter-server state, fixed-point arithmetic, LUTs, DPU/tasklet execution, and timing logs; triangle counting becomes color-triplet graph placement, host-built edge batches, DPU-local MRAM samples, approximate sampling policies, and CLI/Makefile knobs. The shared object is therefore not a simulator command API or a formal IR dialect, but a reproducible host/DPU runtime record that names where data lives, when it moves, what executes on each DPU, and where global state is merged.
object_flow: Entry object: a benchmark, array program, distributed optimization workload, or graph kernel is divided between host code and UPMEM DPU execution.

Binding/rewrite: runtime state names DPU sets, tasklets, MRAM/WRAM placement, transfer granularity, array IDs, per-DPU partitions, host-mediated collectives, synchronization frequency, graph color triplets, and merge points.
Backend handoff: the durable object is runnable host code, DPU kernels, Makefile or CLI parameters, datasets, timing/energy logs, correctness checks, and profiling output.

Boundary: the binding object is real-hardware execution state; keep it distinct from simulator-only PIM APIs and from papers whose central object is a compiler IR rather than a host/DPU runtime record.

clusters.label: PIMeval/PIMsynth DRAM-PIM API and bit-serial compiler line
clusters.analysis: This cluster is best read as a narrow DRAM-PIM API-to-compiler boundary rather than as a general PIM lineage. PIMeval supplies the backend-facing abstraction: C++ API calls over persistent PIM object IDs, allocation/layout state, hardware configs, and command-level timing/energy statistics. PIMsynth then occupies the compiler side of that boundary, lowering bit-parallel combinational Verilog through GenLib/BLIF, a serialized PIM IR-1 dependency graph, LLVM-mediated scheduling and register allocation, and backend code generation into PIMeval API microprograms and tests. The shared object is therefore not a tensor graph or a full memory-hierarchy placement plan, but a simulator-executable command/program boundary where bit-serial operation graphs become auditable row/register/API events.
object_flow: Entry object: a benchmark kernel, PIM data object, or Verilog/GenLib operation enters as a PIM API object or bit-serial logic graph.
Binding/rewrite: compilation lowers the operation through IR, scheduling, assembly, test generation, and microprogram-like command sequences.
Backend handoff: the durable object targets PIMeval-style simulation, command statistics, and validation harnesses.
Boundary: the cluster stays narrow; HARMONI is adjacent but centered on hierarchy mapping rather than PIMeval API command compilation.

clusters.label: LLM-oriented PIM memory-hierarchy mapping and runtime-state modeling
clusters.analysis: This cluster is about LLM execution becoming a memory-hierarchy object before it becomes a conventional compiler IR. HARMONI is the strongest public anchor: it turns Transformer execution into a DFG whose tensors carry address, rank/chip location, row/column access, phase/layer/head, communication, and logic-unit binding metadata, then rewrites mapped work into subtasks plus synchronization and aggregation nodes before simulation. PAPI and Hermes show a different middle object: runtime serving state, where request/token parallelism or hot/cold neuron activity chooses GPU/PIM/NDP placement rather than rewriting a static graph. Ouroboros makes the same pressure visible at wafer scale through token pipeline stages, H-tree placement, KV page tables, bitmaps, row/column-valid registers, and fault-remap state. UniNDP and CIM-MXU are useful boundary cases: they share hierarchy-aware mapping and backend handoff concerns, but their durable objects are DRAM-NDP instruction/layout state and TPU-level CIM-MXU tiling/configuration state, not the same LLM-serving runtime object. The cluster is therefore a workload/state comparison set around placement, hierarchy binding, and runtime memory state, with HARMONI as the artifact anchor and the rest as paper-level or partial-artifact contrasts.
object_flow: Entry object: Transformer or LLM execution is decomposed into phase, layer, tensor, request, token, neuron, or KV/cache state.
Binding/rewrite: mapping binds those objects onto DRAM, NDP units, PIM logic, GPU/NDP placement, wafer/core coordinates, or SRAM-CIM structures.
Backend handoff: the durable object is a placement, movement, batching, synchronization, or remapping plan with latency, energy, or serving-behavior reports.
Boundary: this is a comparison set around runtime and hierarchy state, not one artifact lineage.

clusters.label: Full-stack IR, ISA, and serialized backend boundaries
clusters.analysis: This cluster is best read as a comparison of where CIM stacks let compiler state survive outside the compiler process. CIMFlow exposes a digital-CIM path through graph/config and per-core ISA JSON; PUMA makes a model graph concrete by lowering it into MVMU/core/tile placement, FIFO and register state, and `.puma` instruction streams; OpenCIMTC serializes a YAML CIM-IR whose Attribute, Mapping, and Calculation regions carry layer identity, physical placement, and deployment knobs into generated simulator or hardware-oriented code. CIM-MLC and PIMCOMP strengthen the comparison by showing adjacent boundaries: CIM-MLC's reusable state is distributed across ONNX annotations, Python architecture configs, pass order, and tiered meta-operator outputs, while PIMCOMP centers the array group, mapping info, pseudo-instructions, and pixel-level runtime records. The common point is not a shared IR or research lineage; it is representational visibility at the handoff from graph/mapping decisions to backend execution artifacts.
object_flow: Entry object: a source graph, mapped layer set, model object, or CIM-IR enters a compiler path that keeps intermediate state inspectable.
Binding/rewrite: lowering binds operators to hardware hierarchy, addresses, instruction fields, calculation parameters, or simulator/chip targets.
Backend handoff: the durable object is serialized as JSON, YAML, assembly, meta-operators, generated scripts, or simulator inputs.
Boundary: the cluster is an inspection path across stacks; shared visibility does not imply shared schema, compatibility, or lineage.

papers.title: AccelCIM: Systematic Dataflow Exploration for SRAM Compute-in-Memory Accelerator
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Chenhao Xue, Yukun Wang, An Guo, Yuhui Shi, Jinwei Zhou, Xiping Dong, Yihan Yin, Yuanpeng Zhang, Tianyu Jia, Wei Gao, Qiang Wu, Xin Si, Jun Yang, Guangyu Sun
papers.summary: **AccelCIM** contributes a systematic design-space exploration framework for SRAM compute-in-memory accelerator dataflows, with particular emphasis on the coupling between CIM macro parameters and macro-array organizations. Its strongest stack contribution is in hardware-aware mapping and backend evaluation: the paper models weight-stationary versus output-stationary dataflows, broadcast versus systolic interconnect, macro capacity, pipeline level, and compute-I/O overlap, then ranks candidate designs using cycle-accurate simulation and post-layout PPA extraction through an SRAM-CIM macro compiler, SPICE characterization, RTL generation, Cadence synthesis, and place-and-route. The demonstrated workloads are GEMM-like LLM inference cases, including Qwen3-0.6B, LLaMA-3-8B/70B, and GPT-3 175B, under W8A8 integer computation and prefill/time-to-first-token evaluation. For CIM compiler/IR research, AccelCIM is best read as a **config-and-schedule-centered mapping stack**: it makes hardware dataflow options and macro-array resources first-class, while upstream graph IR, explicit ISA lowering, runtime state, and serialized reusable intermediate states remain outside the demonstrated public interface. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.17692))

papers.title: Accelerating Triangle Counting with Real Processing-in-Memory Systems
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.venue: IPDPS Workshop on Graphs, Architectures, Programming, and Learning (GrAPL); also indexed as arXiv/CoRR abs/2505.04269
papers.authors_or_group: Lorenzo Asquini, Manos Frouzakis, Juan Gómez-Luna, Mohammad Sadrosadati, Onur Mutlu, Francesco Silvestri
papers.summary: PIM-TC is a real-hardware graph-processing stack for triangle counting on UPMEM processing-in-memory DPUs. Its main contribution is a workload-specific host/DPU implementation that maps COO-format graph edges to PIM cores using vertex coloring, constructs host-to-DPU edge batches, optionally applies uniform and reservoir sampling for approximate counting, uses Misra-Gries summaries to handle high-degree vertices, and measures the result on a real UPMEM platform. For CIM compiler/IR research, the work is most useful as evidence for the backend/runtime boundary of a graph workload on commercial digital PIM: it names hardware resources, batching state, graph partition state, and per-DPU memory-resident samples, while the demonstrated reusable abstraction is closer to a specialized mapping/runtime protocol than to a general compiler dialect or instruction-level IR. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.04269v2))
axis_D_rewrite_types: runtime scheduling, placement, batching, or data movement

papers.title: AdaP-CIM: Compute-in-Memory Based Neural Network Accelerator using Adaptive Posit
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Jingyu He, Fengbin Tu, Kwang-Ting Cheng, Chi-Ying Tsui; HKUST
papers.summary: **AdaP-CIM** is best read as a hardware–numeric-format co-design paper for CIM inference rather than as a compiler or IR stack. Its main contribution is **Adaptive Posit**, a Posit-derived format that bounds the regime length with parameter `rs` and adds an exponent-extension encoding mode, paired with a **speculative alignment unit** that computes the maximum effective exponent before CIM MAC execution. The demonstrated scope is BERT-Base-Uncased quantization on three GLUE tasks and synthesis of a 256×64 SRAM-CIM macro in TSMC 28 nm against a Posit/CT-based baseline. For CIM compiler/IR research, the reusable interface is clearest at the numeric type and alignment-state boundary: `AdaP(n, es, rs)`, effective exponent, encoding flag, exponent extension, fraction, `Emax`, and exponent-offset shift become the objects that a future IR would need to name, type, preserve, and lower. ([DATE Conference](https://past.date-conference.com/proceedings-archive/2024/DATA/1077_pdf_upload.pdf))
axis_D_rewrite_types: numeric format, precision, quantization, or approximation

papers.title: An In-Memory Computing Accelerator with Reconfigurable Dataflow for Multi-Scale Vision Transformer with Hybrid Topology
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Zhiyuan Chen; Yufei Ma; Keyi Li; Yifan Jia; Guoxiang Li; Meng Wu; Tianyu Jia; Le Ye; Ru Huang
papers.summary: This DAC 2024 paper contributes a digital SRAM-CIM accelerator architecture and reconfigurable dataflow for static-shape, image-domain multi-scale Vision Transformers whose topology mixes convolutional layers with multi-head attention. Its strongest CIM-stack contribution is at the hardware-mapping and backend-dataflow boundary: the paper highlights MHA pipeline reordering, a two-stage softmax strategy, fused attention matrix multiplication to reduce quadratic intermediate traffic, reconfigurable IMC engines, a distributor network, reuse buffers, accumulation, and post-processing paths. The demonstrated scope is a TSMC 22 nm evaluation against a baseline IMC accelerator, reporting 2.20×–2.52× MHA speedup, 40.6%–74.8% MHA energy reduction, and 44.1%–55.9% EDP reduction for typical multi-scale ViTs. ([ACM Digital Library](https://dl.acm.org/doi/epdf/10.1145/3649329.3658244?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) For CIM compiler/IR research, the paper is most useful as a hardware-software co-design case where the practical “IR-like” object is a reconfigurable dataflow and resource-binding state, rather than a public frontend, verifier, serializable dialect, ISA, or compiler API.
axis_D_rewrite_types: runtime scheduling, placement, batching, or data movement

papers.title: ARCTIC: Agile and Robust Compute-In-Memory Compiler with Parameterized INT/FP Precision and Built-In Self Test
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Hongyi Zhang, Haozhe Zhu, Siqi He, Mengjie Li, Chengchen Wang, Xiankui Xiong, Haidong Tian, Xiaoyang Zeng, Chixiao Chen
papers.summary: **ARCTIC: Agile and Robust Compute-In-Memory Compiler with Parameterized INT/FP Precision and Built-In Self Test** is best read as a physical digital-CIM macro compiler for SRAM-based DCIM. Its main contribution is a parameterized macro-generation flow that accepts hardware-level specifications—timing and area constraints, memory depth and width, mantissa width, exponent width, exponent offset, and INT/FP format—and produces low-level RTL, MarchCIM BIST logic, and placed-and-routed 28 nm macro layouts through commercial EDA flows. The paper strengthens the lower hardware-generation layer of the CIM stack, especially precision-specialized INT/FP macro construction and DCIM-aware BIST, rather than the model-frontend or tensor-mapping layer. For CIM compiler/IR research, ARCTIC is useful as evidence that numeric-format fields, mantissa/exponent placement, BIST test modes, and physical macro hierarchy are important first-class backend objects, even though the paper foregrounds a template/EDA flow rather than a standalone auditable IR. ([DATE Conference](https://past.date-conference.com/proceedings-archive/2024/DATA/399_pdf_upload.pdf))
workloads: No DNN graph-level workload found in checked sources

papers.title: ARES: A Mapping Framework of DNNs towards Diverse PIMs with General Abstractions
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Xiuping Cui, Size Zheng, Tianyu Jia, Le Ye, Yun Liang
papers.summary: ARES is a compiler-mapping framework for placing static DNN tensor operators onto multiple PIM/CIM-like hardware classes. Its main contribution is a pair of reusable abstractions: a compute abstraction that describes a PIM primitive as a tensorized compute equation, and a memory abstraction that encodes bit-level operand placement through mapping matrices and offsets. These abstractions are used to derive a mapping space over tensor binding, loop binding, loop sizes, resource allocation, execution order, and data movement. The demonstrated setting is simulator-backed DNN inference mapping on four adapted platforms: logic-DRAM, logic-SRAM, MVM-ReRAM, and near-DRAM / PIM-HBM-like hardware.

papers.title: AutoDCIM: An Automated Digital CIM Compiler
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Jia Chen, Fengbin Tu, Kunming Shao, Fengshi Tian, Xiao Huo, Chi-Ying Tsui, Kwang-Ting Cheng
papers.summary: AutoDCIM is best classified as a digital-CIM macro compiler rather than a workload compiler or explicit IR stack. Its public contribution is a spec-to-layout hardware generation flow that takes user macro specifications, builds a DCIM macro architecture, and produces an optimized physical layout using template-based generation plus a layout exploration loop over array partitioning choices. The demonstrated stack slice is the hardware-EDA layer: customized SRAM-based digital CIM cells, compute/peripheral structures, array partitioning, and layout generation.
workloads: AI inference / DCIM macro use cases
exact benchmark models: \"Unknown / not found in checked public sources\"

papers.title: C4CAM
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Hamid Reza Farzaneh, Mohammad Hossein Shahrokh Abadi, Minxuan Zhou, Wanqian Zhao, Aviral Shrivastava, Jeronimo Castrillon
papers.summary: C4CAM is a 2024 ASPLOS paper that proposes an MLIR-based compiler and mapping flow for content-addressable-memory accelerators, focused on similarity and search-style workloads rather than general tensor-program compilation. Its main technical contribution is a layered lowering path from TorchScript/Torch MLIR into a generic `cim` abstraction and then into a CAM-specific `cam` abstraction that names CAM hierarchy, allocation, search mode, similarity metric, and partial-result handling. The demonstrated workloads are KNN, hyperdimensional-computing similarity, and DNA read mapping, evaluated through a CAMASim/Eva-CAM-style simulator setup with FeFET TCAM/MCAM assumptions rather than through a public end-to-end compiler artifact. The work is most useful to CIM compiler/IR research as an example of making CAM search and hierarchy mapping explicit in IR, with the clearest reusable boundary around pattern lowering, CAM resource binding, and simulator-facing cost-model integration. ([Cfaed](https://cfaed.tu-dresden.de/publications?pubId=3738))
axis_D_rewrite_types: mode selection across compute, memory, or device classes

papers.title: CIM-MLC: A Multi-level Compilation Stack for Computing-In-Memory Accelerators
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Songyun Qu, Shixin Zhao, Bing Li, Yintao He, Xuyi Cai, Lei Zhang, Ying Wang
papers.summary: CIM-MLC is a CIM compiler/mapping framework that contributes a three-tier hardware abstraction and a matching three-level scheduling pipeline for static DNN inference. Its main abstraction separates **architecture parameters**—chip, core, crossbar, buffer, NoC, ALU, cell precision, ADC/DAC precision—from **computing modes**—Core Mode, Crossbar Mode, and Wordline Mode—so that a compiler can select scheduling strategies at DNN-operator, MVM, or row-activation granularity. The demonstrated stack ingests ONNX graphs, records mapping decisions as graph attributes, performs graph segmentation, duplication, MVM-level pipelining, and WLM data remapping, then emits meta-operator flows for simulator-backed evaluation. For CIM compiler/IR research, CIM-MLC is valuable because it makes hardware-resource granularity explicit and provides a concrete meta-op backend boundary, while also illustrating a common “hidden IR” pattern: much of the reusable semantics sits in ONNX annotations, Python configs, optimization pass order, and codegen templates rather than in a single independently verifiable IR object. ([ar5iv](https://ar5iv.org/html/2401.12428v2))
axis_C_first_class_objects: Core Mode / Crossbar Mode / Wordline Mode

papers.title: CIM-MXU / Leveraging Compute-in-Memory for Efficient Generative Model Inference in TPUs
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Zhantong Zhu, Hongou Li, Wenjie Ren, Meng Wu, Le Ye, Ru Huang, Tianyu Jia
papers.summary: **CIM-MXU** studies how a digital SRAM compute-in-memory matrix unit could replace the conventional systolic MXU inside a TPUv4i-like inference accelerator. Its main contribution is a hardware/software co-design and modeling study: a CIM-MXU microarchitecture built from a systolic grid of CIM cores, a TPU-level architecture model with VMEM/CMEM/HBM-style hierarchy, a mapping engine for Transformer-layer operators, and architecture exploration over CIM-MXU array size and count. The demonstrated workloads are representative generative-model inference kernels, especially GPT-3-30B Transformer-layer evaluation and DiT-XL/2 block evaluation, with INT8 experiments and BF16 support described at the microarchitecture level. For CIM compiler/IR research, the paper is most useful as a hardware-resource and cost-model case study: it shows what a backend would need to know about CIM-core grids, weight-update paths, tiling, memory hierarchy, and precision modes, while the reusable compiler boundary is clearest at the mapping/configuration state rather than at an explicit IR or instruction stream. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.00461))
axis_D_rewrite_types: mode selection across compute, memory, or device classes

papers.title: CIM-Pruner: A Dual-Mode Compute-In-Memory Macro for Efficient VLMs with Intra-Chunk Token Pruning and Merging
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Zhuojun Han, Siqi He, Chixiao Chen, Haozhe Zhu
papers.summary: CIM-Pruner is publicly described as an ISCAS 2026 paper proposing a dual-mode Compute-in-Memory macro for efficient Vision-Language Models through in-memory token pruning and token merging. Its strongest public evidence places the contribution at the CIM macro and narrow hardware-software co-design layer: it targets the token-reduction behavior of VLM inference and appears to make intra-chunk pruning/merging a hardware-visible operation. For CIM compiler/IR research, the main value is not an exposed frontend, IR, mapper, ISA, or reusable backend interface, but the set of dynamic objects it motivates: token masks, merge groups, chunk boundaries, macro mode switches, and token lineage. Those objects are important because a future VLM-oriented CIM backend would need to name, preserve, verify, and lower them across the boundary between token-level algorithms and CIM macro execution. ([Haozhe's Blog](https://zhutmost.com/publication))

papers.title: CIM-Tuner: Balancing the Compute and Storage Capacity of SRAM-CIM Accelerator via Hardware-mapping Co-exploration
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Jinwu Chen, Yuhui Shi, He Wang, Zhe Jiang, Jun Yang, Xin Si, Zhenhua Zhu
papers.summary: **CIM-Tuner** is a hardware-mapping co-exploration stack for SRAM compute-in-memory accelerators. Its main contribution is not a general-purpose compiler IR, but a reusable modeling and mapping abstraction: SRAM-CIM macros are represented as matrix-projection units with parameters such as accumulation length, parallel channels, storage-compute ratio, input-compute bandwidth, and weight-update bandwidth; accelerators are represented as a three-stage template with Input SRAM, CIM macro array, Output SRAM, and external bandwidth; and each operator is assigned a two-level mapping strategy combining spatial scheduling, temporal update priority, and macro-level tiling. papers.summary: **ReSCIM** contributes a fine-grained hybrid CIM architecture that integrates compact MLC ReRAM storage with SRAM-CIM computation, using local ReRAM-to-SRAM weight loading, differential sensing, and folded weight mapping to improve storage density and variation resilience for DNN inference. Its strongest contribution is at the device/cell/macro/accelerator boundary: the paper names a ReSCIM array segment, embeds a 1T1R ReRAM crossbar with an SRAM cell, supports both analog- and digital-based SRAM-CIM computation, and evaluates accelerator-level energy, latency, area efficiency, and inference accuracy, including AlexNet-reported improvements. For CIM compiler/IR research, ReSCIM is best read as a hardware-software co-design case whose “middle representation” is an implicit combination of weight-placement state, MLC encoding, sensing assumptions, and accelerator simulation parameters rather than an explicit graph/loop/ISA compiler stack. ([ACM Digital Library](https://dl.acm.org/doi/epdf/10.1145/3676536.3676751?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

papers.title: SEGA-DCIM: Design Space Exploration-Guided Automatic Digital CIM Compiler with Multiple Precision Support
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Haikang Diao, Haoyi Zhang, Jiahao Song, Haoyang Luo, Yibo Lin, Runsheng Wang, Yuan Wang, Xiyuan Tang
papers.summary: SEGA-DCIM is a digital SRAM-CIM macro-generation and design-space-exploration framework for producing Pareto-frontier DCIM macro designs across integer and floating-point precision choices. Its strongest contribution is the combination of a parameterized synthesizable DCIM architecture, normalized analytical area/delay/energy/throughput models, an NSGA-II-based multi-objective search over macro parameters, and a template-based Verilog/netlist/layout generation flow that relies on user-provided cell/layout resources and commercial EDA tools. The demonstrated workload abstraction is matrix-vector multiplication with static stored weights and streamed input vectors, evaluated mainly through macro-level PPA trends, generated layouts, and comparisons against published DCIM macro results. For CIM compiler/IR research, SEGA-DCIM is most useful as a hardware-generation and backend-cost-model reference: it makes DCIM macro structure and numeric precision choices first-class, while workload graph import, tensor IR, CIM ISA, runtime state, and simulator-facing instruction streams sit outside the demonstrated interface. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.09451)) |

papers.title: SHERLOCK: Scheduling Efficient and Reliable Bulk Bitwise Operations in NVMs
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Hamid Farzaneh; João Paulo C. de Lima; Ali Nezhadi Khelejani; Asif Ali Khan; Mahta Mayahinia; Mehdi Tahoori; Jeronimo Castrillon
papers.summary: **SHERLOCK** is a mapping and scheduling framework for bulk bitwise logic in memristive NVM compute-in-memory systems. Its main compiler contribution is to treat a bulk-bitwise program as a DAG of operand and operation nodes, cluster dependent operations so their operands fit NVM array columns, bind those clusters to row/column resources, and generate simulator-compatible read/write/shift/CIM instructions while accounting for the latency–energy–reliability tradeoff of multi-row activation. The demonstrated flow starts from C bulk-bitwise kernels parsed with pycparser, targets scouting-logic-style ReRAM and STT-MRAM arrays, and evaluates BitWeaving, bit-sliced Sobel, and Usuba-generated bit-sliced AES through an extended gem5 + NVSim + SPICE/statistical-modeling backend. For CIM compiler/IR research, SHERLOCK is best read as a graph-to-hardware-layout compiler whose reusable semantics are clearest in the DAG, cluster/layout state, instruction format, and reliability-aware MRA model. ([cfaed.tu-dresden.de](https://cfaed.tu-dresden.de/files/Images/people/chair-cc/publications/2406_Farzaneh_DAC.pdf))

papers.title: SimplePIM: A Software Framework for Productive and Efficient Processing-in-Memory
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Jinfan Chen, Juan Gómez-Luna, Izzat El Hajj, Yuxin Guo, Onur Mutlu
papers.summary: **SimplePIM** is a C-level programming framework for UPMEM-style processing-in-memory systems that makes PIM-resident arrays, host-managed metadata, communication collectives, and array iterators the primary reusable objects. Its contribution is clearest as a runtime/programming layer: users register or create arrays, move them with scatter/gather/broadcast/allreduce/allgather, and invoke map, general reduction, or zip iterators through host-side calls while the framework handles DPU allocation, alignment, per-DPU partitioning, scratchpad-aware execution, and DPU binary loading. The paper demonstrates this stack on real UPMEM hardware across reduction, vector addition, histogram, linear regression, logistic regression, and K-means, reporting both LoC reduction and runtime comparisons against hand-optimized UPMEM baselines. For CIM compiler/IR research, SimplePIM is most useful as an example of a “hidden IR” embedded in runtime metadata and DPU argument structs: the stack does not center on an explicit graph or dialect, but it does expose a concrete backend contract for naming PIM arrays, preserving placement state, and lowering high-level array operations to real PIM execution. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2310.01893v1))

papers.title: SparseP: Towards Efficient Sparse Matrix Vector Multiplication on Real Processing-In-Memory Systems
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Measurement and Analysis of Computing Systems (POMACS), Vol. 6, No. 1, Article 21; SIGMETRICS/PERFORMANCE 2022 companion presentation
papers.authors_or_group: Christina Giannoula, Ivan Fernandez, Juan Gómez-Luna, Nectarios Koziris, Georgios Goumas, Onur Mutlu
papers.summary: SparseP is a real-system sparse linear algebra study and software library for SpMV on UPMEM near-bank digital PIM. Its concrete contribution is a family of 25 hand-written host/DPU kernels covering CSR, COO, BCSR, and BCOO sparse formats, multiple scalar data types, 1D and 2D matrix partitioning, PIM-core load balancing, tasklet-level load balancing, and synchronization choices, evaluated on a 20-DIMM UPMEM system against CPU and GPU baselines. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2201.05072)) For CIM compiler/IR research, SparseP is most useful as a sparse-mapping and backend-contract case study: it exposes what a compiler would need to represent for real near-bank PIM execution, including sparse format metadata, host–PIM transfer phases, DPU memory placement, tasklet schedules, partial-output merging, and reproducibility constraints around hardware, SDK, and benchmark preparation. The demonstrated abstraction is a library-plus-configuration stack rather than a standalone compiler IR, but its artifact makes the hidden mapping state unusually concrete. ([GitHub](https://github.com/CMU-SAFARI/SparseP))

papers.title: SynDCIM: A Performance-Aware Digital Computing-in-Memory Compiler with Multi-Spec-Oriented Subcircuit Synthesis
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Kunming Shao, Fengshi Tian, Xiaomeng Wang, Jiakun Zheng, Jia Chen, Jingyu He, Hui Wu, Jinbo Chen, Xihao Guan, Yi Deng, Fengbin Tu, Jie Yang, Mohamad Sawan, Tim Kwang-Ting Cheng, Chi-Ying Tsui
papers.summary: SynDCIM is a performance-aware SRAM-based digital CIM macro compiler whose evidenced contribution is a macro-generation EDA flow: a user provides architectural parameters and PPA-oriented performance constraints, the tool forms a subcircuit search space from a characterized library, applies a heuristic multi-spec search, emits RTL/netlists and circuit constraints, and then uses commercial synthesis, APR, timing, DRC/LVS, and post-layout simulation to obtain a layout. The paper strengthens the lower compiler/backend side of the CIM stack: subcircuit libraries, resource-level architectural search, layout-aware backend integration, and silicon validation. The demonstrated workloads are macro-level MAC operations under precision modes such as INT4/8, FP8, BF16, FP4/8, and silicon test modes for a 64×64 MCR=2 macro, rather than a public model-graph frontend or reusable tensor IR. For CIM compiler/IR research, SynDCIM is best read as a hardware-resource/configuration compiler whose “IR-like” semantics live in the macro spec, subcircuit-library choices, timing constraints, and generated RTL/netlist/layout flow. ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2411.16806v2))

papers.title: Towards Floating Point-Based Attention-Free LLM: Hybrid PIM with Non-Uniform Data Format and Reduced Multiplications
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Lidong Guo, Zhenhua Zhu, Tengxuan Liu, Xuefei Ning, Shiyao Li, Guohao Dai, Huazhong Yang, Wangyang Fu, Yu Wang
papers.summary: This paper contributes a hardware-software co-design for attention-free LLM inference that makes the MVM/EWM split in Mamba and RWKV the central optimization boundary. Its clearest CIM-stack contribution is a precision-and-arithmetic layer: PN, a PIM-oriented exponent-free non-uniform weight format that preserves bit-sliced analog RRAM MVM execution, and a multiplication-free approximate FP16 EWM implementation mapped onto a proposed 3D-SRAM digital PIM array. The demonstrated path is not a general compiler IR, but it does expose useful compiler/IR ingredients: a named numeric representation, a group-wise offline parameter-search algorithm, an operator-to-hardware mapping rule, a PN shift configuration boundary, conversion units between INT and FP stages, and simulator/synthesis-backed hardware evaluation for Mamba/RWKV families. The work is therefore best placed as a narrow end-to-end co-design with a strong isolated precision/mapping abstraction, relevant to CIM compiler research as evidence for treating bit significance, scaling-factor propagation, operator sensitivity, and domain transition as first-class lowering state. ([NIC SEFC](https://nicsefc.ee.tsinghua.edu.cn/nics_file/pdf/3b18c2b6-a7c4-439d-9fa8-d6276749f085.pdf))

papers.title: UniNDP: A Unified Compilation and Simulation Tool for Near DRAM Processing Architectures
papers.subtitle: Scoped CIM stack note
papers.authors_or_group: Tongxin Xie, Zhenhua Zhu, Bing Li, Yukai He, Cong Li, Guangyu Sun, Huazhong Yang, Yuan Xie, Yu Wang
papers.summary: UniNDP is best read as a unified mapping-and-simulation framework for **DRAM-based near-data / near-DRAM processing architectures**. Its main contribution is a narrow but concrete stack that takes ML operator descriptions and hardware configuration, enumerates partition and data-layout strategies across DRAM hierarchy levels, prunes and ranks those strategies with DRAM-timing-derived predictors, lowers top candidates to a small NDP instruction abstraction, and evaluates them with an instruction-driven timing simulator. The paper strengthens the CIM/PIM stack at the **mapping, hardware-resource abstraction, cost-model, and simulator-backend** layers rather than at the frontend language or formal IR-verification layer. The demonstrated scope is ML operators from CNN and LLM workloads—especially MM and MVM shapes—on bank-, device-, and rank-level NDP architectures modeled after UPMEM, AiM, HBM-PIM, and DIMMining-like organizations. ([nicsefc.ee.tsinghua.edu.cn](https://nicsefc.ee.tsinghua.edu.cn/nics_file/pdf/d356f1fd-6204-4b21-8849-a9c3b2e15065.pdf))
